evidence: Packages map to clear domain concerns: api, config, exploration, indexer/blacklab, model, query, utils, viz — no historical accidents visible.
Dependency direction is consistently outward from api/exploration toward model/config/query/utils; model is a pure leaf with no upward dependencies.
LongIntHashMap in utils has no visible callers in the reviewed source files — may be dead code or used in a precomputed-collocation loader not included in src/main.
ExplorationResult.seed field encodes both single-seed (lemma string) and multi-seed (comma-joined string) modes in one field — a boundary contract hidden in runtime convention rather than type.

SingleSeedExplorationOptions and ExplorationOptions are separate records with three duplicated field definitions (topCollocates, minLogDice, minShared); toBaseOptions() projection bridges them but the duplication suggests a missing shared boundary.

SemanticFieldExplorer is a public facade delegating entirely to CollocateProfileComparator and MultiSeedExplorer — it owns no algorithm; its primary role is as the API boundary for the exploration subsystem, which is legitimate but means touching SemanticFieldExplorer has a wide blast radius.
No import-time side effects or shared mutable state observed across module boundaries; all coordination is through constructor-injected collaborators.
Top-level packages (api, config, exploration, indexer, model, query, utils, viz) each map to a distinct domain capability — a new engineer can explain why each exists.
model package is a pure leaf: AdjectiveProfile, ComparisonResult, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, Edge, ExplorationResult, RelationEdgeType — no upward dependencies, documented in package-info.java.

config package cleanly separates the loader (GrammarConfigLoader with I/O) from the value object (GrammarConfig) and delegates pattern-building to RelationPatternBuilder — single-responsibility at the package level.

api package has one handler class per endpoint group (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers) with ExploreResponseAssembler owning model-to-JSON translation.

ExplorationResult is a traditional class (with getX() accessors) while most model types are records (AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun) — the package-info.java documents this mixed convention but it reduces predict navigability.
ExplorationResult.seed field is dual-use: single-seed mode stores a lemma string; multi-seed mode stores a comma-joined seed list. This blurs the ownership boundary of the model type.

ExploreResponseAssembler is public final in the api package — its presentation concern is correctly placed, but its public visibility means it could be depended on from outside the api package if the codebase grows.
SemanticFieldExplorer class name suggests domain logic ownership but it is now primarily a facade delegating to CollocateProfileComparator and MultiSeedExplorer.
Java naming conventions are internally consistent: PascalCase classes, camelCase methods/fields, all lowercase package names with underscore separator (word_sketch).

Handler classes in api package are uniformly package-private (no public modifier) — ConcordanceHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, SketchHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers — one consistent pattern.

Mixed accessor naming in model package: traditional class ComparisonResult and ExplorationResult use getX() prefixed accessors; record types AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, Edge use record-style accessors without prefix. This is documented in package-info.java but creates visible inconsistency.

QueryResults.ConcordanceResult interface requires getSentence(), getStartOffset(), getEndOffset(), getDocId() using getX() convention on an interface; records in QueryResults implement these with @Override — consistent within the sealed type hierarchy.

Two parallel options types: ExplorationOptions and SingleSeedExplorationOptions — both are public records in the exploration package with overlapping field names but different field counts. Their Javadoc field names are identical for shared fields.
ExploreResponseAssembler has both a populateExploreResponse() method and a populateComparisonResponse() method — different naming patterns (populate vs format) but not a drift across sibling classes.

RelationUtils uses a non-final class (not final) despite having only static methods and a private constructor — inconsistent with other utility classes (CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator, RelationPatternBuilder) which are all final.
HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling provides a consistent top-level error boundary: IAE → 400, IOException → 500, Exception → 500. All handler methods are wrapped through this.
GrammarConfigLoader throws IOException with descriptive messages for every config error path; wraps in IllegalStateException with actionable startup messages in createDefaultEnglish().
BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor catches bare Exception (not IOException or specific BlackLab exceptions) and wraps in IOException — overly broad catch loses the specific exception type.

CollocateQueryHelper accepts @Nullable BlackLabIndex at construction time (documented as test-only allowance) but defers null detection to individual method calls via Objects.requireNonNull — this means production misuse surfaces as a runtime NPE wrapped in IOException rather than at object construction.

SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles has explicit null check 'if (seedNouns == null) throw new IllegalArgumentException' on a @NonNull annotated parameter — redundant defensive check inconsistent with the rest of the codebase which relies on @NonNull annotations.

SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries catches IOException per relation and adds to a relationErrors list, allowing the sketch to return partial results. This is an intentional 'continue on error' pattern that is not documented at the method level.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti calls HttpApiUtils.sendError(exchange, 400, ...) directly for the seeds.size() < 2 check, bypassing the wrapWithErrorHandling pipeline used for all other 400-class validation in sibling methods.
BlackLabConllUIndexer constructor catches bare Exception and wraps to IOException — same broad catch pattern as BlackLabQueryExecutor.
LogDiceCalculator.compute throws IllegalArgumentException for non-positive inputs, which is appropriate for a pure computation utility.
CollocateQueryHelper.getTotalFrequency catches RuntimeException and wraps to IOException with a descriptive message — acceptable boundary translation.
Domain-aligned class names throughout: SemanticFieldExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator, RelationPatternBuilder, GrammarConfigLoader — all communicate intent clearly.
Method names in exploration subsystem are descriptive: exploreByPattern, compareCollocateProfiles, fetchExamples, computeCollocateIntersection, buildDiscoveredNouns.

Retrieval verb vocabulary is inconsistent across the codebase: getTotalFrequency (query), findCollocations (query), fetchSeedCollocates (exploration), fetchExamples (explorer public API) — three different verbs for 'retrieve from index' without clear distinction between them.

CqlUtils.mergeLemmaConstraint — 'merge' is misleading; the method creates a new constraint incorporating existing xpos/tag constraints, but does not merge two constraints symmetrically. A better name would be injectLemmaIntoConstraint or addLemmaToConstraint.

ExplorationHandlers.buildExploreRequest — 'build' understates that this method also validates seeds, resolves relation config, and parses numeric parameters. The returned ValidatedExploreRequest record name captures the validation aspect well, but the builder method name does not.
BlackLabQueryExecutor.buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution — verbose but specific; 'withLemmaSubstitution' clearly distinguishes this from a generic BCQL builder.
SemanticFieldExplorer.nounCqlConstraint — field name is accurate, though the variable represents a CQL pattern string derived from grammar config; collocateNounPattern would be more precise.
CollocateQueryHelper.OVER_FETCH_FACTOR — clear constant name.

handleSemanticFieldExplore vs handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti — the 'Multi' suffix is appended rather than using the noun-first pattern (handleMultiSeedExplore), creating slight asymmetry with SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed.

QueryExecutor interface has a single production implementation (BlackLabQueryExecutor) with 6 methods. The Javadoc explicitly documents this: 'The single production implementation (BlackLabQueryExecutor) means the interface acts primarily as a testability seam rather than an abstraction boundary.' Honest and useful for testing.

SemanticFieldExplorer is now a thin facade: compareCollocateProfiles() delegates entirely to comparator, exploreMultiSeed() delegates entirely to multiSeedExplorer, fetchExamples() is a 4-line pass-through. The class adds no transformation or policy. It serves as a stable public API, which has value, but the value-add is only naming stability.

ExploreResponseAssembler contains 8 static methods all in one class — some format ExplorationResult, others format ComparisonResult. These are two distinct model types but both serialize to JSON for exploration endpoints. The coupling is justified by single presentation concern.

HttpApiUtils combines response writing and request parameter parsing in one class. The Javadoc argues both serve the 'HTTP layer of the REST API' and the class is package-private. The argument is reasonable but the combination creates a wide utility class.

SingleSeedExplorationOptions and ExplorationOptions have three overlapping field definitions. SingleSeedExplorationOptions.toBaseOptions() is the only projection path. Since Java records cannot extend records, composition is the only option, but the current design chose duplication over composition.

CqlUtils is a genuine shared utility called from both GrammarConfigLoader (token counting) and RelationPatternBuilder (substituteAtPosition, mergeLemmaConstraint) and BlackLabQueryExecutor — the abstraction pays for itself.

RelationPatternBuilder is a pure static utility extracting pattern-building from RelationConfig — appropriate extraction with multiple callers (SemanticFieldExplorer, MultiSeedExplorer, SketchHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers).
LongIntHashMap in utils appears to have no visible callers in the reviewed production source files — potential dead code or unexploited abstraction.
CollocateQueryHelper is package-private and genuinely reduces duplication across 3 methods in BlackLabQueryExecutor — good extraction with real leverage.
pom.xml declares: blacklab-engine 4.0.0, blacklab-query-parser 4.0.0, fastjson2 2.0.57, slf4j-api 2.0.7, logback-classic 1.5.18, JUnit 5.9.0 (test), jspecify 1.0.0.

BlackLab 4.0.0 is resolved from a raw GitHub raw-content URL ('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/instituutnederlandsetaal/BlackLab/mvn-repo/'). The pom.xml comment explicitly warns: 'This URL has no SLA and can become unavailable (rate-limited, branch deleted, or repo renamed).' This is a real CI/CD reliability risk.
Two BlackLab artifacts (engine + query-parser) are expected — they are distinct modules of the same framework, not duplicate libraries for the same purpose.

fastjson2 2.0.57 is used throughout the api package for JSON serialization/deserialization. fastjson (v1) had critical security vulnerabilities; fastjson2 is a rewrite claimed to address them. Version 2.0.57 is relatively recent but the library is not universally recommended for new projects.

com.sun.net.httpserver is used as the HTTP server throughout the api package. This is a JDK internal API (com.sun.*) — technically available in Java 21 but not part of the official public API surface. Java 21 does not restrict access to it, but it can change between JDK versions without notice.
SLF4J + Logback is a standard and well-maintained logging stack — no concern.
jspecify 1.0.0 for null annotations is a lightweight, appropriate choice.
No obvious unused dependencies — all declared artifacts have visible imports in the source.
No version conflicts detectable — version properties are centralized in pom.xml properties block.
No multiple libraries for the same purpose detected (no moment+dayjs style duplication).

CollocateQueryHelper.executeBcqlQuery is well-decomposed into named phases: collectHits() and scoreHits() are private methods with clear single responsibilities; the top-level method reads as a pipeline.
BlackLabSnippetParser is a focused static utility — all methods are pure functions of their inputs with no shared state, clearly scoped to XML/text parsing.

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern is decomposed into four named private helpers (fetchSeedCollocates, buildCollocateToNounsMap, filterByMinShared, identifyCoreCollocates) that map to algorithmic phases — intention-revealing and testable in isolation.

ConlluConverter.convertConlluToWplChunks uses 'writer = null' as a state sentinel after chunk rotation — the Optional<long[]> return from rotateChunkIfNeeded signals rotation and carries a new counter, an unusual return type that creates read friction.

SketchHandlers.handleRelationQueryForPattern hardcodes a limit of 50: 'executor.executeSurfacePattern(lemma, fullPattern, 0.0, 50)'. The sibling method executeAndFormatCollocations uses 20. Neither limit is explained or configurable.
Main.showUsage() contains 35+ sequential System.out.println calls — a literal text dump that could be a single multi-line string constant, creating maintenance surface for trivial output.

BlackLabQueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern: the extractCollocateLemma lambda passed to collectFrequenciesFromGroups has a two-step fallback (try extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition, fall back to extractPlainTextTokenAt) — the fallback cascade is correct but inlined within a lambda, making it less visible.

GrammarConfigLoader.fromReader manually reads a char[] buffer into StringBuilder — idiom is correct but verbose when new BufferedReader(reader).lines().collect(joining()) or reader.transferTo() would be cleaner.

CollocateProfileComparator.buildAdjectiveProfileList: the two-pass variance computation is explicitly commented ('Second pass for variance — requires the mean — unavoidable two-pass for numerical stability') — appropriate documentation of non-obvious algorithmic choice.

SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries uses a BiFunction<RelationConfig, ExecutedSketch, Map<String,Object>> builder parameter for customizing response construction — this is a flexible, low-coupling pattern but the BiFunction type makes the signature harder to read at a glance.
GrammarConfig/GrammarConfigLoader separation is a clean seam: callers receive GrammarConfig (a value object), not GrammarConfigLoader (I/O machinery). The boundary is explicit and one-directional.

QueryExecutor interface defines a clear boundary between query execution and consumers; all methods return QueryResults.* types confined to the query package, avoiding leakage of BlackLab types into higher layers.
Constructor injection of GrammarConfig, QueryExecutor, and SemanticFieldExplorer into api handler classes makes dependencies explicit and the seam testable.
ExploreResponseAssembler owns model-to-JSON translation: all presentation concern for ExplorationResult and ComparisonResult is centralized in one place, with handlers only handling HTTP layer.

ExplorationResult.seed field overloaded for two modes creates a leaky seam: in multi-seed mode, ExploreResponseAssembler.buildEdges(ExplorationResult) passes result.getSeed() as the source node in an Edge — this produces a comma-joined string ('theory,model') as a graph node identifier rather than individual seed nodes.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti: early-return path for 'nouns_per' check (line 121-123) sends 400 via sendError directly, while the subsequent seeds.size() < 2 check (line 129-132) also uses sendError directly — both bypass the wrapWithErrorHandling error pipeline that all other validation in the class uses.

ComparisonResult provides both filter methods (getFullyShared, getPartiallyShared, getSpecific) AND a getSummaryCounts single-pass method. Callers needing both summaries and filtered lists must call both, re-traversing the list.

RelationConfig.computeDeprel() is called multiple times per request in SketchHandlers (once in executeRelationQueries filter, once in buildRelationResponse) — a computed method re-executed on the same object without caching.

GrammarConfigLoader.fromReader manually reads a char buffer in a loop rather than using a standard Reader-to-String idiom — the boundary contract (Reader → String → JSON parse) is correct but the intermediate step is verbose.

ConcordanceHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples: when relation is not found, falls back to a proximity pattern with a logger.warn — the fallback behavior is not communicated to the caller (response includes 'fallback: true' which is good, but the warn level implies something unexpected in a documented fallback path).

SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles has explicit null check 'if (seedNouns == null) throw new IllegalArgumentException("seedNouns must not be null")' on a parameter already annotated @NonNull — classic defensive overengineering on a typed non-nullable value.

CollocateQueryHelper.executeBcqlQuery has inline phase comments ('Phase 1: collect per-hit data', 'Phase 2: score hits', 'Phase 3: rank and limit') that restate what the extracted method names (collectHits, scoreHits) and the sorting step already communicate — restating comments.

GrammarConfigLoader.fromReader manually reads via a char[] buffer loop ('char[] buf = new char[65536]; StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder(); int n; while ((n = reader.read(buf)) != -1) sb.append(buf, 0, n)') — this is a verbose low-level idiom that a Reader.transferTo(Writer) or CharStreams.toString() would replace in 1-2 lines.

BlackLabSnippetParser.extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition contains comment: '// The position < 1 guard is defensive: all current callers pass values from findLabelTokenIndex() which returns >= 1' — this comment justifies a check that the author acknowledges is unreachable, a pattern common in AI-generated code that adds guards then explains why they are technically unnecessary.
Main.showUsage() has 35+ individual System.out.println calls for static text — a boilerplate pattern that suggests copy-paste generation rather than authored code.

WordSketchApiServer constructor 'Objects.requireNonNull(grammarConfig, "grammarConfig must not be null")' while ExplorationHandlers constructor uses 'Objects.requireNonNull(grammarConfig, "grammarConfig must not be null; exploration endpoints require a loaded grammar configuration")' — inconsistent verbosity in null guard messages for the same field type.
CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator, RelationPatternBuilder — all have focused, purposeful Javadocs with concrete examples. No restating comments detected in these utility classes.

BlackLabQueryExecutor method logger.debug calls ('findCollocations: skipping query — lemma is null or empty', 'executeSurfacePattern: skipping query — lemma is null or empty', 'executeDependencyPattern: skipping query — lemma or deprel is null/empty') follow a repetitive pattern across three near-identical guard clauses — boilerplate without centralization.
Edge, ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, RelationEdgeType — concise records/enums with appropriate minimal documentation. No debt detected.
LogDiceCalculator docstring is precise and informative — explicitly defines the formula and range. No restating.

GrammarConfigLoader.java explicitly rejects the old 'cql_pattern' key: canonical field name is 'pattern'; a Javadoc comment states 'cql_pattern is no longer supported' — migration was completed and enforcement added.
GrammarConfigLoader.validateNoLegacyKeys() throws IOException when the deprecated 'copulas' key is present, preventing any stale config from loading — deprecated API actively blocked.
No @Deprecated annotations, no TODO/FIXME referencing 'migrate', 'legacy', 'old api', or 'remove after' found in any seed file.

RelationUtils.RELATION_ALIASES provides backward-compatible short-alias resolution ('adj_predicate' → 'noun_adj_predicates') but this is intentional API convenience, not an incomplete migration — callers use canonical IDs in config and short aliases in HTTP parameters.
No mixed-paradigm patterns (e.g. class+functional, old API+new API coexistence) detected across the seed files or broader codebase.
WordSketchApiServer Javadoc lists legacy alias '/api/semantic-field' alongside canonical '/api/semantic-field/compare' — explicitly documented as 'legacy alias', not a stale shim.

Package layout: api/ (8 files), config/ (5 files), exploration/ (5 files), indexer/blacklab/ (2 files), model/ (10 files), query/ (6 files), utils/ (3 files), viz/ (1 file) — all well within the 10-file flat-directory threshold.

model/ package has 10 files (AdjectiveProfile, ComparisonResult, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, Edge, ExplorationResult, PosGroup, RelationEdgeType, RelationType, SharingCategory, package-info) — at the boundary but all are focused DTOs with no mixed concerns.

ExploreResponseAssembler is declared public in api/ package — it is exclusively consumed by ExplorationHandlers (also in api/) but its public visibility means it could be used by any package; its placement is correct but visibility is wider than necessary.
CollocateQueryHelper is package-private in query/ — appropriate scoping, good boundary discipline.
No root-level straggler files; Main.java is the entry point with high fan-in (referenced by nothing, it references all subsystems), which is appropriate for CLI entry.
The exploration/ package cleanly groups SemanticFieldExplorer (facade), MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator, and their option records — concerns align with package boundary.
viz/ package has a single RadialPlot class — thin but not a problem given it is a distinct rendering concern.
No bidirectional coupling across packages found: api/ → exploration/ → query/ → utils/ is the dependency direction; model/ is a shared dependency with no outbound references outside itself.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti calls HttpApiUtils.sendError(exchange, 400, ...) directly for the seeds.size() < 2 validation, while all other validation in the same and sibling handlers throws IllegalArgumentException which is caught by wrapWithErrorHandling — inconsistent error contract within the same module.

SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles (public) throws IllegalArgumentException for seedNouns.size() < 2, but CollocateProfileComparator.compareCollocateProfiles (package-private) returns ComparisonResult.empty() when seedNouns is null or empty — the two methods have divergent contracts for the same precondition.

SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles has an explicit 'if (seedNouns == null) throw IllegalArgumentException' guard immediately followed by a @NonNull annotation — the annotation already documents non-null intent, the check is redundant but not harmful.

QueryExecutor interface: findCollocations uses 'Find' naming while executeSurfacePattern, executeCqlQuery, executeBcqlQuery, executeDependencyPattern use 'execute' — inconsistent verb convention in interface methods that return similar types.

HttpApiUtils.requireParam documents that it throws IllegalArgumentException — wrapWithErrorHandling is documented to catch this — the contract chain is coherent and consistently applied in SketchHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, and most of ExplorationHandlers.

GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() wraps checked IOException in unchecked IllegalStateException — documented in Javadoc; callers expecting IOException must use load(Path) directly. This is an intentional contract split, documented.

RelationConfig.computeDeprel() returns null when relation type is not DEP or no deprel can be derived — @Nullable annotation is present and the callers (SketchHandlers) guard against null. Contract is correct.

SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles: 'if (seedNouns == null) { throw new IllegalArgumentException(...); }' immediately precedes a @NonNull annotation — the annotation already documents non-null intent, making the explicit check redundant (though harmless).

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern (package-private overload): 'if (seed == null || seed.isEmpty()) { return ExplorationResult.empty(""); }' — the empty-string sentinel is used as the seed in empty result, which is slightly odd but not a dead branch.

BlackLabQueryExecutor.executeDependencyPattern and executeDependencyPatternWithPos both have identical early-exit guards: 'if (lemma == null || lemma.isEmpty() || deprel == null)' followed by logging and empty list return — same guard pattern repeated in two adjacent methods.

CorpusQueryHandlers.parseBcqlRequest: returns null for the 413 case and throws IAE for all other failures — the null return path requires callers to check for null return, which is a clear but slightly unusual control flow (documented in the Javadoc).

BlackLabSnippetParser.extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition: 'if (position < 1) return null' with comment 'The position < 1 guard is defensive: all current callers pass values from findLabelTokenIndex() which returns >= 1' — explicitly documented defensive guard, not unintentional dead code.
No identical if/else branches, no always-true/false conditions, no async-without-await patterns (Java, not async), and no boolean simplification candidates found in core logic paths.
GrammarConfigLoader.deriveTokenPosition: 'if (labelIdx < 0) return defaultPos' followed immediately by token counting loop — clean early-return logic, no dead path.

HTTP response objects throughout the api/ package use raw Map<String, Object> for JSON building (ExploreResponseAssembler, SketchHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers) — no typed response DTOs exist; the type system provides no compile-time verification that required fields are present.

ExploreResponseAssembler.populateExploreResponse and populateComparisonResponse accept Map<String, Object> output maps and mutate them — callers must know which keys will be populated without any type-level contract.
QueryResults.CollocateResult record has @Nullable String collocateLemma — correctly annotated, appropriately nullable.

RelationType enum values include SURFACE, DEP, ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF — but in SketchHandlers, RelationType.SURFACE and RelationType.DEP are used as dispatch keys, while ADJ_PREDICATE etc. appear in the exploration layer. The same enum carries two distinct conceptual groupings (query-dispatch vs. exploration-relation semantics), which weakens its discriminator role.
GrammarConfig.getVersion() is annotated @Nullable — callers in Main.java use it without null guard in System.out.println context, which could produce 'null' in output for legacy configs.

jspecify @NonNull and @Nullable annotations are used consistently on public API boundaries in QueryExecutor, HttpApiUtils, ExplorationHandlers, and SemanticFieldExplorer — good annotation discipline for key interfaces.
PosGroup.fromString returns OTHER as fallback for unknown strings — type-safe handling of unrecognised values.
No raw generic types (raw List, raw Map without type parameters) found in production code.

Flagged duplication between ExplorationHandlers.java:4 and CollocateProfileComparator.java:8 is import block boilerplate — this is not design duplication; both files have distinct responsibilities and no meaningful structural overlap in logic.

SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries uses a callback (BiFunction) to share loop logic between surface and dependency sketches — effective extraction of a repeated structural pattern into a data-driven loop.

ExplorationHandlers.buildExploreRequest is a shared preamble that collapses three repeated parameter-extraction steps (seeds, relation, params) for multi-seed handlers — good extraction; reduces 3-way repetition.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldComparison has a slightly different parameter surface (no 'relation' param, different preconditions) compared to handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti, leading to two similar but not identical handler bodies — not deep nesting, but structural similarity without full extraction.
CorpusQueryHandlers.parseBcqlRequest is cleanly extracted from handleCorpusQuery — appropriate single-responsibility split.
SemanticFieldExplorer acts as a thin facade over MultiSeedExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator — each class has a focused single responsibility, no god-class patterns.
GrammarConfigLoader has two private parse phases (parseAndValidateVersion, validateNoLegacyKeys, parseRelations, parseRelation) each doing one thing — well-decomposed.

BlackLabQueryExecutor.collectFrequenciesFromGroups takes a Function<String, String> lemmaExtractor parameter to share iteration logic across three different grouping strategies — data-driven design that avoids copy-paste.

Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand: constructs BlackLabQueryExecutor, then GrammarConfigLoader.load, then WordSketchApiServer — explicit linear initialization with no hidden ordering constraints; startup failure at any step is handled with early return and cleanup.
WordSketchApiServer constructor defers port binding to start() — construction and network binding are intentionally separated, which avoids the common anti-pattern of I/O in constructors.
HttpApiUtils.setCorsHeader reads System.getProperty("cors.allow.origin", ...) at call time, not at class-load time — correct pattern that allows test overrides via System.setProperty.
GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish reads System.getProperty("grammar.config", ...) at call time — same correct pattern.
No static initializers, class-level DB connections, or module-level network calls found anywhere in the codebase.
SemanticFieldExplorer receives both executor and grammarConfig via constructor injection — no global singletons, no service locator pattern.

BlackLabQueryExecutor opens the file index in its constructor (I/O at construction time) — this is I/O at object creation, not at class loading, which is the more benign form; it is contained within a try/catch and wrapped in IOException.

AtomicReference<WordSketchApiServer> in Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand is used to safely share the server reference between the main thread and shutdown hook — appropriate use for the specific concurrency requirement.
No circular initialization dependencies detected: the dependency graph is Main → WordSketchApiServer → (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ...) → QueryExecutor, GrammarConfig — strictly acyclic.

Core business logic is well unit-tested: LogDiceCalculator, LongIntHashMap, CqlUtils, BlackLabSnippetParser, CollocateQueryHelper, SemanticFieldExplorer, MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator all have dedicated test files.
Handler layer is tested in HandlersTest.java (412 lines) using a MockExchange stub — this tests HTTP routing and parameter parsing but exercises stub executors, not the real index query path.
CorpusQueryHandlers and VisualizationHandlers are covered in HandlersTest.java (grep confirms 16 references to these classes), indicating handlers are integration-tested at the HTTP layer with stubs.

No integration tests that exercise the full stack: HTTP handler → SemanticFieldExplorer → BlackLabQueryExecutor → real BlackLab index. The BlackLabQueryExecutorTest exists but requires an actual index to be present.
BlackLabDummyTest.java (46 lines) appears to be a placeholder test that does not test meaningful functionality.
Main.java (CLI entry point) has no unit tests, which is acceptable for a thin CLI dispatcher.
Test files use stub QueryExecutor implementations consistently rather than mocking frameworks — this avoids test-implementation coupling and is a good pattern.
SemanticFieldExplorerInternalTest provides package-private access to test the 5-param overload — appropriate use of package-level test visibility.
Tests appear to be pure unit tests with stub collaborators; no cross-module verification that the full exploration pipeline produces correct results given a realistic index.
RelationsValidationTest.java tests grammar config validation — good coverage of a critical configuration parsing path.

QueryExecutor interface mixes two verb conventions: findCollocations (find*) vs executeCqlQuery, executeBcqlQuery, executeSurfacePattern, executeDependencyPattern, executeDependencyPatternWithPos (execute*). These methods return comparable types (WordSketchResult or ConcordanceResult) but use inconsistent verbs.

findCollocations(lemma, cqlPattern, minLogDice, maxResults) and executeSurfacePattern(lemma, bcqlPattern, minLogDice, maxResults) have the same signature shape but different parameter semantics: cqlPattern vs bcqlPattern, and findCollocations uses a collocate-constraint-only pattern while executeSurfacePattern expects a full labeled BCQL pattern.
HTTP API parameter naming: single-seed endpoint uses 'seed' (singular), multi-seed endpoint uses 'seeds' (plural), comparison endpoint uses 'seeds' — this inconsistency is visible to API consumers.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldComparison does not accept a 'relation' parameter (intentionally aggregates across all relations), but handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti both accept 'relation' — callers may not understand why 'relation' is ignored on the comparison endpoint.

handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti rejects 'nouns_per' parameter with a 400 error, while handleSemanticFieldExplore accepts it — an API consumer would need to read documentation to know which parameters apply to which endpoint.
Both explore and explore-multi accept: top, min_shared, min_logdice, relation. Both could be unified into a single endpoint with a 'seeds' parameter that accepts either one or many values.

ConcordanceHandlers uses parameter names 'seed' and 'collocate', while ExplorationHandlers uses 'seed' and 'adjective' for the /examples endpoint — 'adjective' is domain-specific while 'collocate' is more general; these are sibling endpoints with parallel purpose but different naming.

QueryExecutor.executeBcqlQuery and executeCqlQuery are both 'execute a pattern, get concordances back' but differ in parser used — this distinction is correct but the names don't communicate the parser difference clearly to callers.

WordSketchApiServer Javadoc explicitly documents the security model: 'the server binds exclusively to the loopback address (127.0.0.1) so it is never exposed on any external network interface. All endpoints are unauthenticated — access control is provided by the host firewall and the loopback-only bind.'

All endpoints are intentionally unauthenticated — this is a single-user local NLP research tool, not a multi-tenant service. The skip criterion 'Public routes explicitly documented as unauthenticated' applies to the entire server.
No auth decorators, middleware, or permission strings exist; there is nothing inconsistent to compare because the security model is uniformly 'no auth'.
CorpusQueryHandlers.handleCorpusQuery accepts arbitrary BCQL patterns via POST — there is no rate limiting or query complexity budget beyond pattern length (1024 chars) and bracket count (20) checks.
The BCQL endpoint has a 65536-byte body size limit and pattern validation (MAX_BCQL_PATTERN_LENGTH, MAX_BCQL_BRACKET_DEPTH) — input validation exists for the highest-risk endpoint.

CORS header is set to 'http://localhost:3000' by default — this is a per-response header, not a middleware, and is configurable via system property. It limits browser-based cross-origin access, which is a relevant control for a local server.
No route registers with different trust levels: all routes are behind the same CORS and loopback binding. No route bypasses the input validation or error-wrapping infrastructure.
The documentation note warns that changing the bind address 'must be accompanied by an authentication or network-level access-control layer' — good documentation of the security assumption boundary.

strengths: Packages align to domain capabilities with clear layering (model → config/query → exploration → api).
No circular dependencies; dependency direction is consistently top-down.
Constructor injection used consistently across the api layer — no shared mutable state between modules.
config package cleanly separates loader I/O (GrammarConfigLoader) from value object (GrammarConfig).
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: Issues are concentrated in two places: a dual-use String field in ExplorationResult that encodes mode via a runtime convention, and duplicated option fields between SingleSeedExplorationOptions and ExplorationOptions that indicate a missing shared boundary.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The layering and dependency direction are sound — a developer can reason about the architecture without tracing runtime behavior. Two structural rough edges (the overloaded seed field and the duplicated options types) reduce the score from the high 70s: they are not architectural violations but do blur boundaries and create maintenance surface. Score placed at 72 reflecting a well-structured codebase with visible but fixable seam design issues.

strengths: Package structure maps cleanly to domain capabilities with no historical-accident directories.
config package achieves clean separation between I/O loading, value objects, and pattern-building across three focused classes.
api package handler decomposition is consistent and predictable — one class per concern.
package-info.java documents the mixed record/class convention in the model package explicitly.
high_level_elegance.issue_character: The main issue is that two model layer abstractions — ExplorationResult and SemanticFieldExplorer — have accumulated dual responsibilities that blur their stated roles; ExplorationResult encodes mode as a string field rather than as a type, and SemanticFieldExplorer is now a thin facade rather than a domain algorithm owner.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The project's decomposition is good at the package level, and a developer new to the codebase can orient quickly. The score is held back by ExplorationResult's overloaded seed field (which violates the principle that a model type's meaning should be clear from its structure) and the semantic mismatch between SemanticFieldExplorer's name and its current function as a facade. These are real navigational obstacles, not style nits. Score placed at 70.

strengths: Java naming conventions (PascalCase, camelCase) are consistent throughout all packages.
Handler class visibility (package-private) is applied uniformly across all api handler classes.
package-info.java explicitly documents the mixed record/class accessor convention in the model package.
Utility class pattern (private constructor + static methods) is used consistently in CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator, RelationPatternBuilder.
convention_outlier.issue_character: The main drift is in the model package, where traditional classes (ComparisonResult, ExplorationResult) and record types mix two accessor naming conventions in the same package, and RelationUtils is the sole non-final utility class among a set of otherwise-final utilities.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The codebase is largely convention-consistent — the Java naming and visibility patterns hold across sibling classes. The score penalty comes from the mixed getX()/record-accessor naming in the model package (which creates predictability friction even when documented) and the RelationUtils non-final outlier. Score placed at 73: mostly consistent with identifiable but bounded outliers.

strengths: HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling provides a clean, testable top-level error boundary shared by all handlers.
GrammarConfigLoader error messages are specific and actionable throughout all failure paths.
LogDiceCalculator has explicit precondition checks with clear exception messages.
Partial-result behavior in SketchHandlers (continue on relation error) is a reasonable design choice.
error_consistency.issue_character: Error handling strategy is inconsistent at integration points: broad Exception catches at constructor boundaries lose specificity, one validation path bypasses the uniform error pipeline, and a redundant null check on a @NonNull parameter signals mixed strategy between annotation-based and runtime-defensive approaches.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The codebase has a well-designed top-level error boundary and consistent IOException-wrapping at I/O boundaries. The score is held at 62 because of three compounding inconsistencies: the broad catch patterns at construction time, the direct sendError bypass of the error pipeline in multi-seed validation, and the redundant null check on @NonNull that signals unresolved strategy. Each issue is individually minor but together they reduce predictability of failure modes.

strengths: Class names are domain-aligned and self-explanatory throughout all packages.
Exploration algorithm method names (exploreByPattern, compareCollocateProfiles, computeCollocateIntersection) clearly communicate their domain purpose.
Record type and field names in model package are precise: AdjectiveProfile.commonalityScore(), DiscoveredNoun.sharedCollocates(), CoreCollocate.coverage().
Constants like OVER_FETCH_FACTOR and FALLBACK_NOUN_CQL_CONSTRAINT are named with intent.
naming_quality.issue_character: The main naming issue is verb inconsistency across retrieval operations (get, find, fetch used interchangeably) and one misleading verb (merge for a non-symmetric operation), neither of which is severe but both reduce discoverability.
naming_quality.score_rationale: The codebase names things well at the class and domain concept level. The verb inconsistency across retrieval operations is the primary deduction — a developer looking for 'how to retrieve collocates from the index' encounters getTotalFrequency, findCollocations, and fetchSeedCollocates with no clear pattern distinguishing them. Score placed at 72: good naming with identifiable but fixable vocabulary inconsistencies.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface is honest about its testability-seam role in Javadoc — not pretending to be an architectural boundary.
CqlUtils and RelationPatternBuilder are genuine shared utilities with multiple callers each.
CollocateQueryHelper is a well-justified package-private extraction reducing duplication in BlackLabQueryExecutor.
HttpApiUtils justifies its combined concern scope explicitly in Javadoc.
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: The primary abstraction issues are SemanticFieldExplorer acting as a zero-value-add facade (all delegation, no policy) and the field duplication between ExplorationOptions and SingleSeedExplorationOptions, where the Java record limitation is worked around by duplication rather than composition.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: Abstractions generally pay for themselves — CqlUtils, RelationPatternBuilder, CollocateQueryHelper, and the QueryExecutor interface all add clear value. Score is held at 65 because SemanticFieldExplorer is a pure pass-through facade with no added policy or transformation, and the options duplication indicates a missing composition step. The LongIntHashMap with no visible callers is a potential dead abstraction adding minor overhead.

strengths: Dependency set is minimal and purposeful — no obvious redundancy or bloat.
Logging stack (SLF4J + Logback) is the standard Java choice.
Version properties are centralized in pom.xml for maintainability.
Test dependencies are correctly scoped (test) and don't bleed into production.
dependency_health.issue_character: The primary risk is supply-chain fragility from the GitHub-hosted BlackLab repository combined with the internal JDK API usage (com.sun.net.httpserver) — neither is a correctness issue today but both represent brittleness that could block builds or require emergency refactoring.
dependency_health.score_rationale: The dependency set is lean and well-chosen for its domain. The score is held at 63 because two risks are present that could materially disrupt the project: the GitHub raw-URL Maven repository for BlackLab (single point of failure for builds with no SLA) and the reliance on com.sun.net.httpserver (an internal JDK API). The pom.xml comment acknowledges the BlackLab risk but provides no mitigation path in the repo itself.

strengths: Algorithm phases are consistently extracted to named private methods across exploration, query, and config packages.
BlackLabSnippetParser is a focused, stateless utility with no defensive sprawl.
Two-pass variance computation is documented with the algorithmic rationale.
CollocateQueryHelper's private record types (CollocateSearch, HitRecord, SeedCollocateData) keep local data grouped without polluting the wider codebase.
low_level_elegance.issue_character: Control flow issues are localized: inconsistent hardcoded limits between sibling methods, a writer=null sentinel pattern in the converter, and verbose showUsage() — these are craft-level issues that add maintenance friction without being correctness problems.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: The implementation internals are generally well-structured with good phase extraction and appropriate helper isolation. The score is held at 67 due to three recurring craft-level issues: inconsistent hardcoded limits that differ between sibling methods, the ConlluConverter sentinel pattern, and the verbose showUsage. These are individually minor but collectively indicate that the implementation layer was not reviewed with the same care as the design layer.

strengths: GrammarConfig/GrammarConfigLoader separation provides a clean I/O vs value-object boundary.
Constructor injection throughout api layer makes integration seams testable and explicit.
ExploreResponseAssembler centralizes model-to-JSON translation, keeping handlers free of presentation logic.
QueryExecutor interface confines BlackLab-specific types behind a clean abstraction layer.
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: The main seam design issues are a dual-use ExplorationResult.seed field that causes incorrect edge source nodes in multi-seed serialization, and inconsistent error propagation patterns at validation boundaries in the multi-seed handler that bypass the uniform error pipeline.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The top-level boundary design (GrammarConfig/Loader separation, QueryExecutor isolation, ExploreResponseAssembler translation) is well-considered. The score is held at 63 because the ExplorationResult seam issue is a functional defect in multi-seed edge graph generation, and two validation paths in the multi-seed handler bypass the established error pipeline, creating unpredictability at the HTTP boundary.

strengths: Core utility classes (CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator, RelationPatternBuilder) have purposeful, non-restating documentation.
Algorithm-explaining comments (variance two-pass rationale, chunk rotation error path explanation) add real insight rather than restating code.
Domain-specific names throughout — no handleData or processItem patterns observed.
Error messages are generally specific and actionable, not generic ('failed').
ai_generated_debt.issue_character: AI-generated debt is present but not pervasive: it concentrates in defensive null checks on @NonNull parameters, phase comments that restate method names, and verbose idioms where concise standard library calls exist.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: The codebase shows moderate AI-generation signals — the redundant null check on @NonNull, the unreachable-guard rationalization comment, the manual char-buffer reader, and the 35-println showUsage are characteristic LLM patterns. However, these are localized to specific methods and do not pervade the domain logic. Score placed at 68: some identifiable AI-debt patterns but the core algorithm implementations are purposeful and well-crafted.

strengths: Completed migration from 'cql_pattern' to 'pattern' field is enforced at load time by throwing instead of silently accepting the old key.
Deprecated 'copulas' JSON key is actively rejected via validateNoLegacyKeys(), preventing stale configs from reaching production.
Legacy HTTP route '/api/semantic-field' is explicitly documented as a legacy alias and points to the same handler as the canonical path — no duplicated logic.
Relation alias map in RelationUtils is a stable, intentional convenience layer, not migration debt.
No stale TODO/FIXME comments referencing old APIs anywhere in the codebase.
incomplete_migration.issue_character: No incomplete migrations detected; the codebase shows evidence of completed migrations with active enforcement guards against reverting to old APIs.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: The codebase earns a high score because every evidence of past migration has been fully concluded and, notably, validated at runtime. The only 'legacy' markers are an intentional HTTP alias and a backward-compatible alias map, both explicitly documented as intentional. A perfect score is withheld only because the legacy HTTP alias adds a surface that callers could unknowingly rely on, making future removal non-trivial.

strengths: Package names accurately reflect contained responsibilities: config, exploration, query, model, api, utils, viz — no ambiguous naming.
Dependency direction flows cleanly from api → exploration → query → utils, with model as a shared leaf; no bidirectional directory coupling detected.
Package-private visibility is used correctly for internal helpers (CollocateQueryHelper, BlackLabSnippetParser, handler classes).
exploration/ package cleanly separates single-seed, multi-seed, and comparison concerns into focused classes.
No flat-directory overload — the largest package (model/ at 10 files) contains exclusively focused data-carrier types.
package_organization.issue_character: The package structure is well-organised; minor issues are limited to one class with overly broad public visibility and the thin viz/ package that could logically merge into api/.
package_organization.score_rationale: The layout scores well because it matches ownership and change boundaries: handler changes stay in api/, query engine changes stay in query/, grammar changes stay in config/. The score is reduced from the high 80s because ExploreResponseAssembler has unnecessary public visibility from a package-private class standpoint, and the viz/ package is a single-class package whose only consumer (VisualizationHandlers) is in api/ — a move into api/ or a sub-package would reduce cross-package coupling without losing clarity.

strengths: The wrapWithErrorHandling / throw IllegalArgumentException contract is clearly established and consistently applied across SketchHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, and most of ExplorationHandlers.
@Nullable annotations are present on all methods that can return null (computeDeprel, extractLemmaFromMatch, extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition), accurately reflecting the implementation.

GrammarConfigLoader deliberately exposes two factory methods with different exception contracts (load throws checked IOException; createDefaultEnglish wraps in unchecked) and documents both in Javadoc.
QueryExecutor interface documents every method's contract, null-return conditions, and exception types.
contract_coherence.issue_character: The main contract defect is a bifurcated error-handling approach in ExplorationHandlers where one validation path sends a 400 directly while all sibling validations throw IllegalArgumentException, breaking the module-level error contract; a secondary issue is divergent handling of the same precondition (seeds < 2) across two layers.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: Most of the codebase honors stated contracts well — annotations match implementations, exception types are consistent in most places. The score is reduced because two concrete defects exist: the sendError/throw inconsistency in ExplorationHandlers (which would surprise a maintainer adding a new validation) and the public/private contract divergence in the exploration layer for the same seeds < 2 precondition. These are not catastrophic but represent real contract surface that callers must understand separately.

strengths: Consistent use of early-return guard clauses in all query methods reduces nesting and makes intent clear.
Explicitly documented defensive guards (e.g., extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition comment) distinguish intentional defensive checks from accidental dead code.
No dead code after unconditional returns, no always-true/false conditions found in any reviewed file.
Boolean simplification candidates are absent — the codebase avoids verbose if/return True/else/return False patterns.
logic_clarity.issue_character: The clarity issues are limited to a handful of redundant null checks that duplicate annotations and a repeated guard pattern across two adjacent methods; no true dead code paths or logic contradictions were found.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: Logic is generally clear and provable. The redundant null check in compareCollocateProfiles and the duplicated guard in dependency methods are the main detractors — they don't introduce bugs but add noise. The unusual null-return contract in parseBcqlRequest is documented but adds a non-standard control flow pattern that a reader must notice. Overall, the codebase scores well for logic clarity with no serious defects found.

strengths: jspecify @NonNull/@Nullable annotations are consistently applied on all public QueryExecutor, HttpApiUtils, and exploration method signatures.
Sealed interface QueryResults.ConcordanceResult with permitted subtypes (SnippetResult, CollocateResult) provides compile-time exhaustive pattern matching capability.
Record types throughout model/ and exploration/ packages provide immutable, strongly-typed data carriers with auto-generated accessors.
No raw generic types found; all collections are parameterized.
PosGroup enum with fromString fallback provides type-safe handling of string-to-enum conversions.
type_safety.issue_character: The main type safety weakness is the pervasive use of Map<String, Object> for all HTTP response assembly, which means the type system cannot verify that mandatory response fields are populated; a secondary issue is the dual-purpose RelationType enum that conflates two orthogonal concerns.
type_safety.score_rationale: Java's compile-time type system gives this codebase a structural advantage over dynamically-typed languages. The annotation discipline on public API boundaries is good. However, the complete absence of typed response DTOs for API output means the JSON shape of every endpoint is a runtime convention rather than a compile-time contract — a maintainer adding a new field cannot rely on the compiler to find all the places that need updating. This pulls the score into the upper-60s rather than the 80s.

strengths: SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries callback pattern eliminates repetitive surface/dep sketch loops and makes the variation point explicit.
SemanticFieldExplorer is a proper facade with no logic of its own, delegating to focused collaborators.
GrammarConfigLoader is cleanly decomposed into small, single-purpose private methods with no over-long methods.
BlackLabQueryExecutor.collectFrequenciesFromGroups centralises grouping iteration with an injected extractor function, avoiding three near-identical loops.
ExploreResponseAssembler is a pure data-transformation class with no HTTP or business logic — clean single responsibility.
design_coherence.issue_character: The boilerplate duplication flagged by the mechanical detector is limited to import blocks and is a false positive; the genuine design concern is that ExplorationHandlers contains three similar explore-response-building methods that share structure but are not fully unified due to parameter set differences.
design_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase shows mature design instincts: callback-based loops, facade delegation, and single-method classes all appear in the right places. The score is held in the mid-70s because the exploration handlers have a family of three similar handler methods (explore, explore-multi, compare) that partially share structure via buildExploreRequest and buildBaseExploreResponse but the comparison handler does not participate in the shared preamble — this asymmetry creates a maintenance surface where a parameter change to the shared params must be applied in some but not all handlers.

strengths: All dependencies are passed via constructor injection; no global singletons or service locators.
System property reads are deferred to call time, not class-load time, enabling test overrides without JVM restart.
WordSketchApiServer separates construction from network binding (start() method), following the principle of deferring side effects.
Shutdown hook uses AtomicReference to safely coordinate between threads without requiring synchronization.
Main.java shows explicit linear initialization order with early-exit error handling at each step.
initialization_coupling.issue_character: No significant initialization coupling issues found; the codebase consistently applies constructor injection and defers side effects to explicit lifecycle methods.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: This is a well-structured Java application with clean initialization discipline. The only deduction is that BlackLabQueryExecutor performs file I/O in its constructor (opening the index), which is not a boot-order coupling issue but does mean that any test creating this class must have a real index file. This is a minor testability concern rather than a coupling defect. The overall initialization path is linear, fully explicit, and easy to reason about.

strengths: All major utility classes (LogDiceCalculator, CqlUtils, LongIntHashMap, BlackLabSnippetParser) have dedicated, comprehensive unit tests.
Exploration logic (SemanticFieldExplorer, MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator) is tested with realistic stub data that exercises the scoring algorithms.
Handler tests use a lightweight MockExchange rather than spinning up a full HTTP server, making them fast and deterministic.
Grammar config loading is validated by dedicated tests (GrammarConfigLoaderTest, RelationsValidationTest) covering error cases.
test_strategy.issue_character: Unit test coverage of pure logic is strong, but there is a gap in end-to-end integration tests: no test exercises the HTTP handler → query engine → real index pipeline, and one placeholder test file adds noise without coverage.
test_strategy.score_rationale: The test suite demonstrates good unit test discipline for business logic, which is the most valuable coverage area. The score is held in the mid-60s because: (1) BlackLabDummyTest appears to be a non-test placeholder; (2) there are no integration tests that would catch regressions in the serialization of real BlackLab query results through the API layer; (3) the full exploration pipeline is only tested against stub data, meaning subtle scoring bugs that depend on real frequency distributions would not be caught.

strengths: HTTP endpoints are RESTful GET/POST with clear path naming (/api/sketch, /api/semantic-field/explore, /api/relations).
Error responses consistently use JSON { 'error': message } format via HttpApiUtils.
CORS handling is uniform across all endpoints via the registerHandler wrapper.
Parameter defaults are consistent: 'top' defaults to 10, 'min_logdice' to 3.0 across all exploration endpoints.
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: The API surface has two coherence problems: the QueryExecutor interface mixes find*/execute* naming conventions for comparable operations, and the HTTP API has parameter name inconsistencies (seed vs seeds, adjective vs collocate) that would confuse API consumers building clients.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The HTTP API's path naming and response shapes are generally well-designed, but parameter naming inconsistencies across sibling endpoints reduce the predictability of the API surface. The QueryExecutor interface is the more impactful issue for developers — the find/execute inconsistency means readers must parse documentation to understand that findCollocations and executeSurfacePattern return the same type but work very differently. A developer adding a new method to the interface has no clear naming convention to follow.

strengths: Security model is explicitly and prominently documented in WordSketchApiServer Javadoc, including the condition under which it would need to be changed.
Loopback-only binding is enforced in code (InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress()), not just documentation.
All endpoints share the same uniform (no-auth) trust model — there are no inconsistencies between route siblings.
BCQL endpoint has input validation guards (pattern length, bracket depth, body size) — the highest-risk user-controlled input point is the best protected.
CORS origin is configurable at runtime via system property, allowing controlled expansion without code changes.
authorization_consistency.issue_character: Authorization consistency is not applicable as a defect category because the entire application deliberately and documentedly has no authentication; the only meaningful finding is an absence of rate limiting on the BCQL query endpoint.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: The score is intentionally not 100 because while the uniform no-auth model is coherent and documented, the BCQL endpoint accepts arbitrary corpus queries with only length and complexity bounds — no rate limiting or query budget prevents a local process from saturating the index with repeated expensive queries. This is a minor concern for a single-user research tool but would become a real issue in any shared deployment scenario. The documentation of the security boundary assumption prevents a false sense of security.

issues.summary: ExplorationResult.seed encodes single-seed vs multi-seed mode via a runtime string convention
issues.suggestion: Introduce an explicit mode distinction: either add a boolean multiSeed flag and a List<String> seeds field to ExplorationResult, or create separate SingleSeedExplorationResult and MultiSeedExplorationResult types. Update ExploreResponseAssembler.buildEdges to iterate individual seeds rather than using the raw seed string.
evidence: ExplorationResult.seed is used as a single lemma in single-seed mode and as a comma-joined string ('theory,model,hypothesis') in multi-seed mode (MultiSeedExplorer line 75: String.join(',', seeds)).
ExploreResponseAssembler.buildEdges(ExplorationResult) uses result.getSeed() as the edge source node — in multi-seed mode this emits a comma-joined string as a graph node identifier.
ExplorationResult.seeds() parses the field via seed.split(',') — the caller must know which mode produced the result to interpret the field correctly.

issues.summary: SingleSeedExplorationOptions duplicates three fields from ExplorationOptions instead of composing or extending
issues.suggestion: Change SingleSeedExplorationOptions to hold an ExplorationOptions base field and add only the nounsPerCollocate field, replacing the duplicated accessors with delegation. Since records cannot extend records in Java, use composition: record SingleSeedExplorationOptions(ExplorationOptions base, int nounsPerCollocate).
evidence: ExplorationOptions has: int topCollocates, double minLogDice, int minShared.
SingleSeedExplorationOptions repeats all three fields plus adds int nounsPerCollocate — field Javadocs are copy-pasted.
SingleSeedExplorationOptions.toBaseOptions() bridges via projection, but callers must be aware of the two types.

issues.summary: ExplorationResult.seed field overloaded to represent both single-seed lemma and multi-seed comma-joined string
issues.suggestion: Add explicit mode distinction: a List<String> seeds field for the seed list and a boolean multiSeed accessor, or create typed subclasses SingleSeedExplorationResult and MultiSeedExplorationResult. Remove the seeds() split-on-comma workaround.
evidence: ExplorationResult.getSeed() Javadoc: 'Single-seed mode: contains the lemma string. Multi-seed mode: contains the seeds joined with a comma.' — dual meaning on one field.
ExplorationResult.seeds() parses the field via seed.split(',') to recover individual seeds, requiring callers to know which mode produced the result.
ExploreResponseAssembler.buildEdges(ExplorationResult) uses result.getSeed() as edge source — produces a comma-joined string as graph node in multi-seed mode.

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer name implies domain algorithm ownership but the class is now entirely a delegation facade
issues.suggestion: Rename to SemanticFieldService or SemanticFieldFacade to signal its role as a coordination entry point rather than an algorithm owner, or document it explicitly as the public API facade for the exploration subsystem in its class Javadoc.
evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles delegates entirely to comparator.compareCollocateProfiles().
SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed delegates entirely to multiSeedExplorer.computeCollocateIntersection().
SemanticFieldExplorer.fetchExamples is a 4-line pass-through to executor.executeBcqlQuery() with a stream transform.
Only exploreByPattern contains non-trivial internal logic; even that defers to private helpers.

issues.summary: model package mixes getX() accessors (ComparisonResult, ExplorationResult) with record-style accessors (AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun) in the same package
issues.suggestion: Convert ComparisonResult and ExplorationResult to records (if computed accessors can be expressed as default methods or companion utilities), or document the distinction more prominently. If conversion is not feasible, ensure new model types consistently follow one convention.
evidence: ComparisonResult.getNouns(), getAllAdjectives(), getFullyShared() — getX() convention.
AdjectiveProfile.adjective(), nounScores(), presentInCount() — record convention without get prefix.
ExplorationResult.getSeed(), getSeedCollocates(), getDiscoveredNouns() — getX() convention.
CoreCollocate.collocate(), sharedByCount(), totalNouns() — record convention.

issues.summary: RelationUtils is not declared final despite being a static utility class, unlike all sibling utility classes
issues.suggestion: Add 'final' to RelationUtils and LogDiceCalculator class declarations to match CqlUtils and RelationPatternBuilder and signal that these utility classes are not designed for extension.
evidence: RelationUtils: 'public class RelationUtils { private RelationUtils() {} ... }' — no final modifier.
CqlUtils: 'public final class CqlUtils { private CqlUtils() {} ... }' — final.
LogDiceCalculator: 'public class LogDiceCalculator' — also not final, same pattern.
RelationPatternBuilder: 'public final class RelationPatternBuilder' — final.

issues.summary: BlackLabQueryExecutor and BlackLabConllUIndexer catch bare Exception at construction, losing specific failure context
issues.suggestion: Catch the specific BlackLab exception types (e.g. InvalidQuery, or the checked exception from BlackLab.open()) and translate each to a descriptive IOException. Add an 'index open error type' note in the exception message (e.g. 'corrupt index', 'permission denied') to preserve failure context.
evidence: BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor: 'catch (Exception e) { throw new IOException("Failed to open index: " + e.getMessage(), e); }' — catches Exception broadly.
BlackLabConllUIndexer constructor: 'catch (Exception e) { throw new IOException("Failed to create index: " + e.getMessage(), e); }' — same pattern.
Callers cannot distinguish a permission error from a corrupt index from a configuration error based on the IOException type alone.

issues.summary: handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti calls sendError() directly for seeds.size() < 2, bypassing the uniform IAE → 400 pipeline
issues.suggestion: Replace the direct sendError call with 'throw new IllegalArgumentException("Multi-seed exploration requires at least 2 seeds; received " + seeds.size())' to match the pattern used everywhere else in the class and in handleSemanticFieldComparison.
evidence: handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti line ~130: 'HttpApiUtils.sendError(exchange, 400, "Multi-seed exploration requires at least 2 seeds...")' — direct error send.
All other 400-class validation in the same class uses 'throw new IllegalArgumentException(...)' which wrapWithErrorHandling intercepts.
handleSemanticFieldComparison line ~178: 'throw new IllegalArgumentException("Comparison requires at least 2 seed nouns...")' — uses the uniform pattern for an equivalent check.

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles performs an explicit null check on a @NonNull annotated parameter
issues.suggestion: Remove the explicit null check on seedNouns — the @NonNull annotation is the declared contract. If runtime enforcement is desired, use Objects.requireNonNull() consistently across all @NonNull parameters (or rely on annotation-processing null analysis tools).
evidence: compareCollocateProfiles signature: 'public @NonNull ComparisonResult compareCollocateProfiles(@NonNull Set<String> seedNouns, @NonNull ExplorationOptions opts)'
Method body line ~313: 'if (seedNouns == null) { throw new IllegalArgumentException("seedNouns must not be null"); }' — redundant check.
No other public method in the codebase performs explicit null checks on @NonNull parameters — this is a localized inconsistency.

issues.summary: Retrieval operations use three different verb prefixes (get, find, fetch) without distinguishing semantics
issues.suggestion: Adopt a consistent retrieval verb: use 'find' for corpus queries that return result lists (findCollocations, findSeedCollocates, findExamples), and 'get' only for cheap in-memory accessors. Document this convention in the project's CLAUDE.md or a code conventions note.
evidence: QueryExecutor: findCollocations(), getTotalFrequency() — 'find' vs 'get' for corpus queries.
SemanticFieldExplorer: fetchSeedCollocates(), fetchExamples() — 'fetch' for corpus retrieval.
CollocateQueryHelper: getTotalFrequency() — 'get' for a corpus query that has I/O cost.
No documented convention for when to use get vs find vs fetch in the codebase.

issues.summary: CqlUtils.mergeLemmaConstraint uses 'merge' for a one-sided injection operation, not a symmetric merge
issues.suggestion: Rename to injectLemmaConstraint or addLemmaToToken to accurately reflect the one-directional nature of the operation.
evidence: mergeLemmaConstraint takes an existingConstraint and a lemma, and produces a new constraint prepending lemma="..." while preserving xpos/tag constraints from the existing one.
'Merge' implies symmetric combination of two inputs; here the lemma is injected into (not merged with) the existing constraint structure.
The output always leads with lemma="..." regardless of what was in existingConstraint — it is a replacement/injection, not a merge.

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer adds no behavior, policy, or translation — it is a zero-value delegation facade
issues.suggestion: Either (a) collapse SemanticFieldExplorer into the api layer as a coordinator that directly calls CollocateProfileComparator and MultiSeedExplorer, or (b) have SemanticFieldExplorer add real policy (e.g. parameter validation, logging, caching) to justify its existence as a facade. Document the facade role explicitly if it is retained for API-stability reasons.
evidence: compareCollocateProfiles: 'return comparator.compareCollocateProfiles(seedNouns, opts.minLogDice(), opts.topCollocates())' — pure delegation.
exploreMultiSeed: 'return multiSeedExplorer.computeCollocateIntersection(seeds, relationConfig, opts.minLogDice(), opts.topCollocates(), opts.minShared())' — pure delegation.
fetchExamples: builds bcqlQuery from RelationPatternBuilder then calls executor.executeBcqlQuery() — 4-line pass-through with no abstraction benefit.

issues.summary: SingleSeedExplorationOptions duplicates three fields from ExplorationOptions instead of composing
issues.suggestion: Use composition: record SingleSeedExplorationOptions(ExplorationOptions base, int nounsPerCollocate). Delegate to base.topCollocates(), base.minLogDice(), base.minShared() or keep the projection method. This eliminates the field duplication and makes the relationship explicit.
evidence: ExplorationOptions: record ExplorationOptions(int topCollocates, double minLogDice, int minShared).

SingleSeedExplorationOptions: record SingleSeedExplorationOptions(int topCollocates, int nounsPerCollocate, double minLogDice, int minShared) — topCollocates, minLogDice, minShared duplicated verbatim.
toBaseOptions() constructs a new ExplorationOptions from the duplicated fields — bridging duplication rather than eliminating it.

issues.summary: LongIntHashMap utility class has no visible callers in production source — potential dead abstraction
issues.suggestion: Verify whether LongIntHashMap has any active callers. If it is dead code, remove it. If it is used in a precomputed-collocation loader not yet in src/main, document that dependency in the class Javadoc.
evidence: LongIntHashMap is a non-trivial custom data structure (open-addressing hash map with linear probing, ~100 lines).
No import or usage of LongIntHashMap found in any other reviewed production Java source file.
It may be used in precomputed-collocation loading code not visible in the current source tree, or may be dead code.

issues.summary: BlackLab dependency resolves from a GitHub raw-content URL with no SLA — build will fail if URL becomes unavailable
issues.suggestion: Mirror the BlackLab JARs to a private Maven registry (GitHub Packages, Artifactory, Nexus) and update the repository URL to the mirror. Alternatively, commit the JARs to the repository and resolve via a local file repository. At minimum, document the install-blacklab.sh step as a required first-time setup in README.md.
evidence: pom.xml repository entry: 'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/instituutnederlandsetaal/BlackLab/mvn-repo/'
pom.xml comment explicitly acknowledges: 'This URL has no SLA and can become unavailable (rate-limited, branch deleted, or repo renamed).'
No local install script is set as the primary mitigation — install-blacklab.sh is mentioned as an option but not enforced.

issues.summary: API layer uses com.sun.net.httpserver — a JDK internal API not part of the public API surface
issues.suggestion: Replace com.sun.net.httpserver with a stable embedded HTTP server such as Javalin, Undertow, or Jetty. These are available as Maven Central dependencies, provide a public API, and reduce future JDK migration risk. If keeping the current approach, add a note to the README about the Java version requirement and its implications.
evidence: WordSketchApiServer imports: 'com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpServer', 'com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpHandler' — com.sun.* prefix indicates JDK internal API.
HttpApiUtils imports 'com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpExchange', 'com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpHandler'.
All 5 handler classes use com.sun.net.httpserver types in method signatures.
While functional on Java 21, this API is not guaranteed stable between JDK versions.

issues.summary: Hardcoded result limits differ between sibling methods in SketchHandlers with no explanation
issues.suggestion: Extract the limit to a named constant (e.g. DEFAULT_SKETCH_RESULTS = 20 and SINGLE_RELATION_RESULTS = 50, with comments explaining the distinction) or unify to a single configurable limit. At minimum add a comment explaining why the specific-relation query needs a higher limit than the full-sketch query.
evidence: handleRelationQueryForPattern: 'executor.executeSurfacePattern(lemma, fullPattern, 0.0, 50)' — limit 50.
executeAndFormatCollocations: 'executor.executeSurfacePattern(lemma, fullPattern, 0.0, 20)' — limit 20.
Both methods execute the same underlying query type for the same handler class — the 2.5x difference in limit is unexplained by comments or configuration.

issues.summary: ConlluConverter uses writer=null as a state sentinel for chunk rotation, creating fragile control flow
issues.suggestion: Extract the chunk-rotation logic into a small ChunkWriter wrapper class that internally tracks the current writer and handles rotation. The wrapper's write() method would handle chunk creation transparently, eliminating the null sentinel and the Optional<long[]> signaling return.
evidence: convertConlluToWplChunks: after rotateChunkIfNeeded, 'writer = null' is set as a signal that the next line should open a new chunk.
rotateChunkIfNeeded returns Optional<long[]> as a 'did rotate?' signal carrying the new chunk counter — a non-standard use of Optional.
The null sentinel pattern requires the outer loop to check 'if (writer == null)' before writing — state spread across the calling loop rather than encapsulated.

issues.summary: Main.showUsage() has 35+ sequential System.out.println calls that could be a single text block
issues.suggestion: Replace the sequential println calls with a single System.out.println(""" ... """) text block (Java 15+ syntax, supported in Java 21). This consolidates the static text into a single maintainable unit.
evidence: showUsage() method contains approximately 35 individual System.out.println() calls for a static usage text block.
Java text blocks (triple-quoted strings, available since Java 15) would allow the same output as a single string constant with no runtime overhead.
Multiple adjacent println calls create a maintenance burden — adding a new option requires finding the right position in the sequence.

issues.summary: ExploreResponseAssembler.buildEdges uses result.getSeed() as edge source, producing comma-joined string in multi-seed mode
issues.suggestion: In multi-seed mode, iterate result.seeds() and add one SEED_ADJ edge per individual seed node. Either fix in ExploreResponseAssembler.buildEdges by checking for multi-seed mode, or fix the ExplorationResult.seed field to store seeds as a List<String> (see also arch-dual-seed-field).
evidence: ExploreResponseAssembler.buildEdges(ExplorationResult): 'edges.add(new Edge(result.getSeed(), colloc.getKey(), colloc.getValue(), RelationEdgeType.SEED_ADJ))'
In multi-seed mode, result.getSeed() returns 'theory,model,hypothesis' (MultiSeedExplorer line 75: String.join(',', seeds)).
The graph edge source node becomes 'theory,model,hypothesis' — a comma-joined string rather than individual seed nodes, which is semantically incorrect for a graph visualization.

issues.summary: Two validation checks in handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti call sendError() directly, bypassing the uniform error pipeline
issues.suggestion: Replace 'HttpApiUtils.sendError(exchange, 400, ...)' with 'throw new IllegalArgumentException(...)' in handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti to use the same pipeline as handleSemanticFieldComparison and all other validation in the api layer.
evidence: Line ~121: 'throw new IllegalArgumentException("nouns_per is not supported...")' — uses IAE, consistent with pattern.
Line ~129-132: 'HttpApiUtils.sendError(exchange, 400, "Multi-seed exploration requires at least 2 seeds")' — direct sendError, bypasses wrapWithErrorHandling.
handleSemanticFieldComparison performs an equivalent seeds.size() < 2 check via 'throw new IllegalArgumentException(...)' on line ~178 — the parallel handler uses the uniform pattern.
