issues.summary: ComparisonResult requires separate traversals for summary counts and filtered lists, forcing callers to traverse the adjectives list multiple times
issues.suggestion: Consider having getFullyShared/getPartiallyShared/getSpecific delegate to a single partition call (Collectors.partitioningBy equivalent for 3 categories) and cache the result lazily in ComparisonResult. Alternatively, expose a single partitioned view: record Partitions(List<AdjectiveProfile> fullyShared, List<AdjectiveProfile> partiallyShared, List<AdjectiveProfile> specific).
evidence: ComparisonResult has getFullyShared(), getPartiallyShared(), getSpecific() (each streaming adjectives independently) AND getSummaryCounts() (single-pass).
ExploreResponseAssembler.populateComparisonResponse calls result.getSummaryCounts() for counts AND result.getAllAdjectives() for serialization — two traversals for the same data.
A caller needing all three filtered lists (fully/partially/specific) runs three separate streams over the same collection.

issues.summary: SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles adds explicit null check on @NonNull annotated parameter — defensive overengineering
issues.suggestion: Remove the explicit null check. The @NonNull annotation is the declared contract. If runtime enforcement is needed uniformly, adopt Objects.requireNonNull() consistently in all @NonNull parameter methods, but do not mix annotation-contract and explicit-check strategies.
evidence: Method signature: 'public @NonNull ComparisonResult compareCollocateProfiles(@NonNull Set<String> seedNouns, @NonNull ExplorationOptions opts)'
Method body: 'if (seedNouns == null) { throw new IllegalArgumentException("seedNouns must not be null"); }' — check on a @NonNull typed value.
No other public method in the codebase performs an explicit null guard on @NonNull parameters; this is a localized inconsistency.

issues.summary: CollocateQueryHelper.executeBcqlQuery inline phase comments restate extracted method names rather than adding insight
issues.suggestion: Remove the inline 'Phase N:' comments. The extracted method names (collectHits, scoreHits) and the final sort step are self-documenting. If phase structure documentation is desired, add a single block comment before the method explaining the three-phase design.
evidence: Line ~195: '// Phase 1: collect per-hit data and accumulate collocate frequencies' immediately above 'List<HitRecord> hitRecords = collectHits(...)'
Line ~199: '// Phase 2: score hits with logDice' immediately above 'List<...> scored = scoreHits(...)'
Line ~202: '// Phase 3: rank and limit' immediately above a stream sort — the sort step is self-explanatory.
The extracted method names collectHits and scoreHits already communicate the phases; the comments add no information beyond what the names convey.

issues.summary: GrammarConfigLoader.fromReader uses a verbose manual char-buffer reading loop where a standard 1-2 line idiom exists
issues.suggestion: Replace the manual char-buffer loop with: 'var sb = new StringBuilder(); char[] buf = new char[8192]; int n; while ((n = reader.read(buf)) != -1) sb.append(buf, 0, n);' is acceptable if staying dependency-free, but a cleaner option is wrapping in BufferedReader and using lines().collect(joining(System.lineSeparator())).
evidence: fromReader: 'char[] buf = new char[65536]; StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder(); int n; while ((n = reader.read(buf)) != -1) sb.append(buf, 0, n);'
Java standard library idiom: 'new java.io.BufferedReader(reader).lines().collect(java.util.stream.Collectors.joining("\n"))' or Guava CharStreams.toString(reader) — both are 1-2 lines.
The manual loop is correct but verbose, introducing accidental complexity without performance benefit at config-file scale.

issues.summary: Main.showUsage() contains 35+ sequential System.out.println calls for static text — LLM boilerplate pattern
issues.suggestion: Replace the sequential println calls with a single System.out.println(""" <text> """) Java text block constant defined as a static final String. This eliminates ~33 lines of boilerplate and consolidates the usage text for easier maintenance.
evidence: showUsage() method body consists entirely of ~35 sequential System.out.println("...") calls.
Java text blocks (available since Java 15, supported in Java 21) would express the same output as a single string constant.
This style is a characteristic LLM generation pattern: each line of output generated as a separate println rather than composed as a text block.

issues.summary: viz/ is a single-class package whose only consumer is in api/
issues.suggestion: Staged plan: (1) Move RadialPlot.java into api/ package (or a new api/viz/ sub-package); (2) update import in VisualizationHandlers; (3) validate with: mvn test -pl . to confirm no other callers. This removes the one-file cross-package edge while keeping the class co-located with its sole consumer.
evidence: RadialPlot.java is the only file in viz/; VisualizationHandlers in api/ is its only caller.
The rendering concern is exclusively consumed by the HTTP handler, creating a thin wrapper package with a single cross-package edge.

issues.summary: handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti sends 400 directly instead of throwing IllegalArgumentException like all sibling handlers
issues.suggestion: Replace the direct sendError call with 'throw new IllegalArgumentException("Multi-seed exploration requires at least 2 seeds; received " + seeds.size())' to be consistent with all other 400-class validation in this module. The wrapWithErrorHandling wrapper will catch it and send the 400 response.
evidence: Line ~99 in ExplorationHandlers: 'if (seeds.size() < 2) { HttpApiUtils.sendError(exchange, 400, ...); return; }' — bypasses wrapWithErrorHandling.
All other validation in ExplorationHandlers and sibling handler classes throws IllegalArgumentException which wrapWithErrorHandling catches and maps to 400.
The module-level contract documented in HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling is: 'Catches IllegalArgumentException and sends a 400 response' — direct sendError calls break this contract.

issues.summary: Public and package-private comparison methods have divergent contracts for seeds < 2 precondition
issues.suggestion: Align CollocateProfileComparator.compareCollocateProfiles to throw IllegalArgumentException for size < 2 (or delegate precondition checking entirely to the public facade and document that the package-private method trusts its caller). The current split means the inner method silently returns empty for a case the outer method explicitly rejects.
evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles throws IllegalArgumentException when seedNouns.size() < 2.
CollocateProfileComparator.compareCollocateProfiles returns ComparisonResult.empty() when seedNouns is null or empty — a different contract for the same precondition.
A caller of the package-private method (were one added) would get empty results rather than an exception for the 1-seed case, while the public facade throws.

issues.summary: Redundant null check immediately before @NonNull annotation in compareCollocateProfiles
issues.suggestion: Remove the explicit null check; rely on the @NonNull annotation for documentation and static analysis enforcement. If runtime protection is desired for untyped callers, replace with Objects.requireNonNull(seedNouns, "seedNouns must not be null") which is a more idiomatic pattern and consistent with how other methods in this class use requireNonNull.
evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles(Set<String> seedNouns, ...) is annotated @NonNull on the parameter, then immediately checks 'if (seedNouns == null) throw new IllegalArgumentException(...)' — the check can never be false for a correctly-annotated caller.

issues.summary: Identical null/empty guard pattern duplicated across two adjacent dependency query methods
issues.suggestion: Extract the shared guard into a private helper: 'private boolean isInvalidDependencyInput(String lemma, String deprel)' that centralises the check and log message. This avoids logic drift if the guard condition is updated in one method but not the other.
evidence: executeDependencyPattern and executeDependencyPatternWithPos both contain: 'if (lemma == null || lemma.isEmpty() || deprel == null) { logger.debug(...); return Collections.emptyList(); }' — identical guard logic with identical log messages.
The only difference is executeDependencyPatternWithPos adds a requireNonNull check for headPosConstraint after the guard.

issues.summary: All HTTP response objects use Map<String, Object> rather than typed response DTOs
issues.suggestion: Introduce typed response record classes for each endpoint's response shape (e.g., ExploreResponse, SketchResponse, ComparisonResponse). These can be serialised to JSON by fastjson2 without manual map building. This allows compile-time verification of field presence and eliminates string-key typo risks. Prioritise ExploreResponseAssembler first as it has the most complex response shape.
evidence: ExploreResponseAssembler.populateExploreResponse takes Map<String, Object> and puts 'seed_collocates', 'discovered_nouns', 'core_collocates', 'edges', etc. — any field name typo is a silent runtime bug.
SketchHandlers builds response maps with manual string keys: response.put("status", "ok"); response.put("lemma", lemma); etc.
ExplorationHandlers.buildBaseExploreResponse builds a Map with 'status', 'relation_type', 'parameters' — no compile-time verification that required fields are present.

issues.summary: RelationType enum conflates query-dispatch semantics (SURFACE, DEP) with exploration-relation semantics (ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF)
issues.suggestion: Split into two enums: QueryMode (SURFACE, DEP) for the query-dispatch layer, and RelationCategory (ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF) for the exploration layer. Each context can then switch exhaustively on its own type. Existing code in SketchHandlers, RelationUtils, and the exploration classes would each be updated to use the appropriate enum.
evidence: RelationType.java declares: SURFACE, DEP, ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF — all in one flat enum.
SketchHandlers uses RelationType.SURFACE and RelationType.DEP as query-dispatch keys.
SemanticFieldExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator use RelationType.ADJ_MODIFIER, ADJ_PREDICATE, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF for exploration-relation selection.
A switch statement on RelationType that handles SURFACE/DEP would silently miss ADJ_PREDICATE etc., and vice versa — the type provides no compile-time grouping.

issues.summary: Comparison handler does not use the shared buildExploreRequest preamble, creating inconsistent parameter extraction paths
issues.suggestion: Document the intentional split in a comment on handleSemanticFieldComparison: the handler intentionally skips relation resolution because comparison is cross-relational. Extract the shared top/min_shared/min_logdice parameter extraction into a standalone resolveBaseParams method that all three handlers call, separating it from the relation-specific resolveRelationConfig. This makes the commonality explicit and the intentional difference (no relation for comparison) visible.
evidence: handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti both call resolveRelationConfig and resolveExplorationParams via buildExploreRequest (or inline equivalent).

handleSemanticFieldComparison independently calls HttpApiUtils.requireParam for 'seeds', then resolveExplorationParams — it does not call resolveRelationConfig (intentionally, since comparison aggregates across all relations), creating a structural asymmetry in how parameters are extracted across the three handlers.
If resolveExplorationParams gains a new parameter, the comparison handler must be updated separately from the shared preamble.

issues.summary: BlackLabQueryExecutor performs file I/O in its constructor, coupling object creation to filesystem availability
issues.suggestion: This is an accepted and well-documented pattern for resource-backed classes in Java; the I/O is in a constructor, not at class load time, and is contained within try/catch. Document in the Javadoc that construction requires a valid index path and may throw IOException — the existing signature already declares this. No structural change is needed unless a factory method (BlackLabQueryExecutor.forTesting(...)) is desired for unit tests.
evidence: BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor calls BlackLab.open(new File(indexPath)) — this performs actual filesystem access at object creation time.

Any test that needs a BlackLabQueryExecutor must either provide a real index or mock the BlackLab.open call — the QueryExecutor interface exists as a seam for this, but the concrete class is not independently testable without the filesystem.

issues.summary: BlackLabDummyTest.java is a placeholder test file providing no meaningful coverage
issues.suggestion: Either delete BlackLabDummyTest.java if it adds no value, or expand it to test a specific BlackLab API contract (e.g., index-open behaviour, error handling for a missing index path). If it is kept as a learning scaffold, annotate it clearly with @Disabled and a comment explaining its purpose.
evidence: BlackLabDummyTest.java is 46 lines — likely a placeholder from early development with no substantive assertions about real BlackLab behaviour.
A placeholder test file adds confusion about test intent and may hide that a critical path is untested.

issues.summary: No integration test exercises the full HTTP → exploration → real-index pipeline
issues.suggestion: Add a small integration test corpus (e.g., 100-sentence CoNLL-U snippet) to src/test/resources/. Create an integration test that: (1) indexes the snippet using ConlluConverter + BlackLabConllUIndexer, (2) runs a known query via BlackLabQueryExecutor, (3) asserts that expected collocates appear in the top results. This would catch regressions in the full pipeline without requiring the production 74M-sentence index.
evidence: All handler tests use stub QueryExecutor implementations; WordSketchApiServerTest.java binds a real server but still uses a stub executor.
BlackLabQueryExecutorTest likely requires a real index and may be skipped in CI if no index is available.
The collocate scoring pipeline (executeSurfacePattern → collocate frequency grouping → logDice ranking) has no end-to-end test validating that a known corpus produces expected top collocates.

issues.summary: QueryExecutor interface mixes find* and execute* verb conventions for comparable operations
issues.suggestion: Standardise on 'query*' or 'execute*' prefix for all interface methods. Rename findCollocations to executeCollocations (or queryCollocations) to signal it is a query operation with the same lifecycle as executeSurfacePattern. Update all callers (SemanticFieldExplorer.fetchSeedCollocates, CollocateProfileComparator, etc.).
evidence: findCollocations(lemma, cqlPattern, minLogDice, maxResults) → List<WordSketchResult>
executeSurfacePattern(lemma, bcqlPattern, minLogDice, maxResults) → List<WordSketchResult>
Both return List<WordSketchResult>, both take a lemma + pattern + minLogDice + maxResults signature shape, but use different verb prefixes with no naming convention to distinguish them.

issues.summary: HTTP API uses 'seed' (singular) vs 'seeds' (plural) vs 'adjective' vs 'collocate' inconsistently across sibling endpoints
issues.suggestion: Standardise parameter names across all exploration endpoints: use 'seeds' (plural) consistently and accept comma-separated values (with a single value being the single-seed case), making the explore and explore-multi endpoints unifiable. For the examples endpoints, align 'adjective' and 'collocate' — prefer 'collocate' as the more general term. Document the parameter contract in a shared API reference.
evidence: /api/semantic-field/explore: parameter 'seed' (singular noun)
/api/semantic-field/explore-multi: parameter 'seeds' (plural)
/api/semantic-field/compare: parameter 'seeds' (plural)
/api/semantic-field/examples: parameters 'adjective' and 'noun' (domain-specific names)
/api/concordance/examples: parameters 'seed' and 'collocate' (different naming for analogous concepts)

issues.summary: BCQL query endpoint has no rate limiting; expensive queries could saturate the local index under repeated calls
issues.suggestion: For a local single-user tool this is low priority. If shared deployment is ever planned: add a simple in-memory rate limiter (e.g., a token bucket per source IP) in the registerHandler wrapper in WordSketchApiServer, and document the per-minute query limit. Since the server already binds to loopback, this is a hardening measure rather than a security requirement.
evidence: CorpusQueryHandlers validates pattern length (<= 1024 chars) and bracket count (<= 20) but has no per-client request rate limiting or concurrent-query cap.

BlackLabQueryExecutor executes queries synchronously on the server thread executor; the HttpServer uses null executor (default single-threaded virtual threads or platform thread pool depending on JVM).
A web UI or automated script could submit hundreds of BCQL queries per second, potentially causing index contention.
